cataloging_subject.label: The Destruction of Jerusalem (short version, Pepys 2014)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous short version of the Middle English rhyming-couplet romance now usually called The Destruction of Jerusalem or Titus and Vespasian, as transmitted imperfectly in Cambridge, Magdalene College, Pepys MS 2014 and represented by Rudolf Fischer's two-part 1903-1904 edition and University of Michigan CME item CME00072. The record includes Fischer's brief German closing commentary because the XML includes it, but distinguishes that editorial prose from the medieval poem. It does not identify this work with the separate alliterative Siege of Jerusalem, with Latin or Old English texts titled Vindicta Salvatoris, with the long version of this romance, with the complete Pepys miscellany, or with the digital and repository artifacts that represent it.

catalog_summary.title: The Destruction of Jerusalem (short version, Pepys 2014)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: The wider romance is dated 1375-1400; the short recension is first attested here in a late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century manuscript; first published in two installments, 1903-1904
catalog_summary.region: London for the wider romance; the short recension's separate formation is not securely localized; Pepys MS 2014 was copied in Northamptonshire

titles.value: The Destruction of Jerusalem (short version, Pepys 2014)
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, following the current Middle English Texts Series title while distinguishing the short recension and its base witness
notes: The base Pepys copy has no verified medieval title at this locus. The parenthetical qualifier is modern discovery language, not a manuscript rubric.

titles.supplied_by: Kara L. McShane and Mark J. B. Wright
notes: The editors explicitly describe this as an editorial title for the wider work tradition; their own edition uses the long version.

This historical editorial title should not collapse the poem into the distinct Latin or Old English Vindicta Salvatoris traditions. Fischer's own closing note reclassified the text as a partial version of the Vengeaunce/Battle tradition.
Fischer presents this as a name for the wider poem, not as a verified Pepys rubric.
This name is not evidence that the work is the separate alliterative Siege of Jerusalem.

titles.supplied_by: Two later seventeenth-century hands above the Pepys text, as reported by Fischer

scope.description: Later attribution heading over the manuscript text

notes: Fischer rejects the heading as a work identification; it is retained as reception and attribution history, not a preferred title.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English poet of The Destruction of Jerusalem
notes: A descriptive identity for the unknown medieval author, not a claim that long and short recensions were necessarily made in one authorial act.

agents.name: A. Reinke
notes: Fischer identifies her as a senior schoolteacher in Berlin but supplies no expanded forename.

agents.name: B. Kiehl
notes: Fischer supplies only an initial and surname.

scope.description: The two-part 1903-1904 immediate source edition represented by CME00072

notes: Editorial responsibility does not make Fischer the medieval author.

scope.description: Working transcript made at Cambridge for Fischer's edition
Post-transcription collation against Pepys MS 2014 for Fischer's edition

assertion.notes: Fischer explicitly names Kiehl as collator after Reinke's copying; the statement does not describe the surviving physical form or whereabouts of Kiehl's collation record.

date_statements.display: 1375-1400 for the wider romance; the short recension's separate date is not independently established

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance dates the work, while modern scholarship distinguishes long and short versions. The interval is not a direct date for the act that formed the short recension.

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century, in Cambridge, Magdalene College, Pepys MS 2014

assertion.notes: The broad normalized bounds represent the verbal catalog range; they are not a precise palaeographic dating.

date_statements.display: 1903-1904, first published by Rudolf Fischer in two Archiv installments

assertion.notes: The first installment explicitly says zum erstenmal herausgegeben; the edition was completed in the next volume.
The specialist database localizes the wider romance to London; it does not separately localize the formation of the short recension.
This is the manuscript's reported place of origin and must not be transferred to composition.

form_statements.description: The cataloged medieval work is a rhyming-couplet poem; two brief German prose paragraphs are retained editorial framing rather than a substantive prose component of the medieval work.
Octosyllabic rhyming couplets, with substantial metrical irregularity noted by later editors.
Two German prose paragraphs by Fischer.

subject_statements.label: Christian anti-Judaism, antisemitism, and anti-Jewish violence
notes: This discovery heading records and contextualizes harmful polemical content. It does not endorse the poem's collective blame, supersessionism, dehumanization, or violence against Jews.

subject_statements.label: Divine vengeance, punishment, and Christian supersession
notes: The subject statement describes the poem's ideology rather than accepting its theology or historical claims.

subject_statements.label: Conversion, baptism, relics, and miraculous healing

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English romance recasts the Roman destruction of Jerusalem as Christian divine vengeance for Christ's death. The surviving Pepys short-version text begins after its lost or omitted opening, recounts portents and the martyrdom of James, the illnesses and conversions of Vespasian and Titus, Veronica's image-bearing cloth, the Roman siege, famine and cannibalism, Josephus's escape, and Titus's healing, then breaks off before the wider romance's conclusion. The poem repeatedly blames Jews collectively and uses graphic anti-Jewish violence, torture, dehumanization, and supersessionist theology; those features are documented here for responsible discovery and study, not repeated as historical truth or endorsed. The encoded unit concludes with two German prose paragraphs in which Fischer comments on the partial version and its relation to the wider Vengeaunce tradition.

CME00072 encodes Fischer's poem text from printed pp. 289-298 in Archiv 111 and pp. 25-45 in Archiv 112, followed by the two German paragraphs at the bottom of p. 45. It omits Fischer's edition introduction on pp. 285-288. The first installment ends with a continuation notice; the second calls itself the conclusion, although the Pepys witness and poem are textually imperfect.

The current title and short-version classification come from later scholarship. Fischer's source title Vindicta Salvatoris is retained as an alternate historical edition title. The work is related to but not identical with Latin and Old English Vindicta Salvatoris texts, and it must not be confused with the separate alliterative Siege of Jerusalem.

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 elements: one poem division with 27 editorial verse groups and 3,114 L elements, and one commentary division with two prose paragraphs. Thirty-one PB elements represent printed pp. 289-298 and 25-45. Fischer says the partial version lacks the first 812 verses; the current Database of Middle English Romance says it lacks 814. Those source-lineation claims are preserved as an unresolved discrepancy rather than subtracted from the XML count.

content_parts.label: The Destruction of Jerusalem, short-version text from Pepys MS 2014
content_parts.summary: The imperfect 3,114-line Middle English couplet text printed by Fischer across two journal installments.
notes: The 27 XML verse groups reflect editorial grouping and initials, not a claim for 27 authorial chapters or stanzas.
The first and final textual losses or omissions are not quantified beyond the competing source statements.

content_parts.label: Fischer's closing commentary
content_parts.summary: Two German paragraphs declining full linguistic and stylistic analysis, identifying the printed text as a partial version of the wider Vengeaunce/Battle poem, reporting then-known witness counts, and referring to Bergau and Suchier.

related_works.title: The Destruction of Jerusalem, or Titus and Vespasian
notes: Modern scholarship distinguishes short and long versions. The cataloged Pepys text belongs to the short version; the modern METS edition prints the long version.

related_works.title: The Siege of Jerusalem
notes: A separate alliterative Middle English poem on related material. Similar titles and shared subject matter do not establish identity.

related_works.title: Vindicta Salvatoris
notes: One source tradition used in the composite Vengeance of Our Lord narrative. Fischer's title does not make the Middle English poem a direct one-to-one translation.

related_works.title: Mors Pilati, qui Jesum condemnavit
notes: Fischer names the Pilate-death tradition in his source discussion; later source analysis treats the romance as a compilation from multiple Latin and vernacular traditions.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME source XML for CME00072
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00072, the source title, Middle English and German content, continuous 3,114-line poem, printed-page boundaries, and closing commentary.

evidence.citation: Local structural inspection of CME00072.xml and comparison with the two Fischer scans
evidence.locator: 2 DIV1, 2 HEAD, 27 LG, 3,114 L, 31 PB, 743 MILESTONE, and 2 P elements
evidence.summary: Verifies the poem/commentary boundary, XML metrics, page runs pp. 289-298 and 25-45, omitted introduction pp. 285-288, and inclusion of the p. 45 German commentary.

evidence.citation: Rudolf Fischer, 'Vindicta Salvatoris,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 111 (1903), 285-298
evidence.locator: article pp. 285-298 / BookReader leaves n313-n328 / PDF pages 301-314; encoded text pp. 289-298 / PDF pages 305-314
evidence.summary: Direct inspection verifies the edition title and first-publication claim; Pepys 2014's old and current shelfmarks, contents and folio boundaries; Reinke's transcript, Kiehl's collation, Fischer's editorial practices, source discussion, and the first 1,030 poem lines.

evidence.citation: Rudolf Fischer, 'Vindicta Salvatoris,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 112 (1904), 25-45
evidence.locator: article pp. 25-45 / BookReader leaves n39-n59 / PDF pages 39-59
evidence.summary: Direct inspection verifies continuation through line 3,114 and Fischer's closing commentary, including the partial-version classification, competing 812-line opening-loss count, wider-work titles, then-known witness count, and malformed Suchier coordinates.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Titus and Vespasian and Cambridge, Magdalene College, MS Pepys 2014
evidence.locator: IMEV 1881; composition and form; Pepys ff. 23r-35v; date, origin, contents, and imperfection
evidence.summary: Identifies the work as Titus and Vespasian, dates the wider work 1375-1400 and localizes it to London, describes rhyming couplets and romance subjects, and identifies Pepys 2014 as a parchment Northamptonshire witness of the end of the fourteenth or start of the fifteenth century, imperfect in 3,114 lines and lacking the opening 814 lines.

evidence.citation: Kara L. McShane and Mark J. B. Wright, eds., The Destruction of Jerusalem, or Titus and Vespasian (Medieval Institute Publications, 2021; digital edition 2024)
evidence.locator: edition description; Introduction, especially title, versions, antisemitism, and Manuscripts and Editions
evidence.summary: Supplies the current editorial title, anonymous authorship, Middle English poetry and genre terms, contextualizes the work's antisemitism and violence, distinguishes short and long versions, identifies P as Pepys 2014 ff. 23r-35v, and confirms Fischer used P as base manuscript.

evidence.citation: Walther Suchier, review of Fritz Bergau, Untersuchungen über Quelle und Verfasser des mittelenglischen Reimgedichts The vengeaunce of goddes deth, Archiv 108 (1902), 199-206
evidence.locator: printed pp. 199-206 / BookReader leaves n223-n230 / PDF pages 213-220
evidence.summary: The exact Suchier review corrects Fischer's printed citation coordinates and analyzes the wider Vengeaunce's multiple Latin and French source traditions while distinguishing the alliterative Siege; the inspected review does not name Pepys 2014.
Binds descriptive claims to the separately modeled repository artifact, U-M encoding, two Fischer installments, working transcript, Pepys witness, complete volume scans, and downstream scholarship.

open_questions.question: Why does Fischer say the Pepys partial version lacks the first 812 verses while the current Database of Middle English Romance says it lacks 814 lines?
next_steps: Compare the short-version Pepys text against the current long-version lineation and Phyllis Moe's version study; identify whether the difference reflects editions, couplet counting, or a corrected alignment.

open_questions.question: When and where was the short recension formed, as distinct from the broader romance's 1375-1400 London composition and the Northamptonshire copying of Pepys 2014?
next_steps: Review the full version study and stemmatic scholarship; do not transfer either broad-work composition or witness-copying place to the recension without explicit evidence.

open_questions.question: What additional codicological, scribal, and provenance facts does the current printed Pepys medieval-manuscript catalog provide for MS 2014?
next_steps: Consult McKitterick and Beadle, Catalogue of the Pepys Library, V.i, or ask the Pepys Librarian for the exact entry and any corrections; the College's current guide says there is no online Pepys catalog.

notes: The preferred title follows current scholarship but adds a witness/version qualifier so the viewer does not present Fischer's historically misleading title as an identity claim.
The catalog summary retains German because CME00072 includes Fischer's German commentary; German is not a language of the medieval poem.
The anti-Judaism and antisemitism terms provide necessary content warning and research discovery. They describe the poem's rhetoric and violence, not Jewish history.

Fischer's first installment and the modern witness catalog disagree with his 'thirteenth-century' subtitle: current scholarship dates Pepys 2014 to the end of the fourteenth or start of the fifteenth century and the wider work to 1375-1400.
